title: Ethnicity, Nationalism and Minority Rights – Edited by S. May, T. Modood and J. Squires
abstract: May, S., Modood, T., and Squires, J. (eds) Ethnicity, Nationalism and Minority Rights Cambridge University Press 2004 261 pp. £45 (hardback) £19.99 (paperback) This is an edited collection of essays on a topic of great importance (minority rights in democracies) and many of the chapters are written by leading commentators; Pieterse, Brubaker, Kymlicka, Young, Parekh, and Calhoun prominent amongst them. The editors have aimed for an interdisciplinary exchange, between sociology and politics, which will encourage further debate on multiculturalism, ethnicity, minority rights, and democracy. For the most part the book is successful; the individual contributions generally address the core themes of the book and several of the chapters are outstanding (the chapters by Pieterse and Brubaker are particularly strong). This said, it is not clear why a book which aims to be representative of contemporary debates is not able to find room for a chapter on the changing nature of citizenship, or for that matter why themes such as the politics of identity and the plurality of community cannot be accommodated, as they too have an important bearing on both multiculturalism and the rights of minorities. Several of the chapters interrogate the concepts central to an investigation of minority rights. Rogers Brubaker deals with the hitherto under-theorized concept of ‘the group’, the existence and meaning of which is frequently assumed but rarely investigated. The idea of an ethnic group is a common sense notion but can lead us to essentialize ethnicity: the tendency to partition the world in this way, ‘is a key part of what we want to explain, not what we want to explain things with’ (p. 52). For Brubaker, ethnicities should not be conceived as fixed entities but ‘in relational, processual, dynamic, eventful and disaggregated terms’ (p. 53). Will Kymlicka looks at ways in which minority nationalism is addressed in different parts of Europe. The preference in the West (which historically has favoured suppression or eradication of minority expressions of nationalism in the project of nation-building) is now to frame the claims of minorities within a discourse of rights and justice, thereby placing the issue of minority rights at the heart of debates on contemporary democracy both at home and abroad. In the East of Europe, in contrast, minority nationalisms are frequently associated with threats to national security, and, when framed in this way it becomes impossible to guarantee justice for minorities. According to Kymlicka, there is a real need to ‘desecuritize’ minority issues. The state must be prepared to debate the question of federalism and secession without seeing these as a fundamental threat to security. The possibility of secession does not have to amount to ‘national death’ (p. 167). The framing of the debate in this way, i.e. nation-states versus national minorities, lacks an important dimension. The global context within which minorities (and indeed nation-states) operate and which can provide the ideological raw material, motivation, and indeed justification for their programmes of autonomy, is not considered by Kymlicka. One could argue that the dynamics of minority nationalism, its mobilization, resourcing and communicative networks cannot be understood outside of the global framework of rights and norms which have given such momentum to minority nationalisms in the postwar period. The invitation to take up these themes is declined by Calhoun in his chapter on post-national belonging, which focuses instead on the ‘mobility gap’ between cosmopolitans and nationals. His primary concern is with the individuating tendency within cosmopolitanism, and he makes the very pertinent point that cosmopolitan appeals to humanity are presented in individualistic terms, and in doing so ‘privilege those with the most capacity to get what they want by individual action’ (p. 248). This is indeed a ‘cosmopolitan conundrum’ but one which needs to be offset against the propensity of cosmopolitans to construct new communities of sentiment, anxiety, and belonging in a way which cuts across existing territorial polities. All of which points to the complexity of contemporary debates on democracy, multiculturalism, and minority rights. The editors of this book have provided us with the means to enter these debates with confidence.
